coredata.dc:title: Efficient degradation of antibiotics by heterojunction photocatalysts driving peroxydisulfate activation under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Qi
Bai, Chang-Wei
Sun, Yi-Jiao
Liu, Yu-Jie
Hou, Kun-Jie
Chen, Fei
coredata.dc:description:
Heterogeneous iron materials show potential for activating peroxydisulfate (PDS). However, these systems may be constrained by low efficiency and secondary pollution. The visible-light-assisted activation has been demonstrated as a feasible and effective approach to enhance PDS activation by iron-based materials. In this study, a visible-light-responsive Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 heterojunction photocatalyst was synthesized to improve the ability of Fe2O3 for PDS activation through an effective Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. Rapid tetracycline (TC, a common antibiotic) removal (91.7% within 60 min at a concentration of 20 mg·L-1) was achieved in the constructed Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system under conditions of 0.3 g·L-1 PDS and 0.3 g·L-1 catalyst. The corresponding pseudo-first-order rate constant reached 4.16 × 10-2 min- 1, which was 8.42 and 16.38 times higher than those of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/vis and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS systems, respectively. Moreover, the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 exhibited favorable stability and reusability even after five cycles. Lastly, a catalytic mechanism for TC removal and PDS activation was proposed based on quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests. The SO4 •
- and •OH played a major role in TC removal, while •O2
– and 1O2 contributed as auxiliary factors. This study presents a promising strategy for activating PDS using cost-effective and stable Fe(III)-based heterojunctions, and such an idea can be used to design catalysts for other high-value reactions.

head.ce:title: Efficient degradation of antibiotics by heterojunction photocatalysts driving peroxydisulfate activation under visible light irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Heterogeneous iron materials show potential for activating peroxydisulfate (PDS). However, these systems may be constrained by low efficiency and secondary pollution. The visible-light-assisted activation has been demonstrated as a feasible and effective approach to enhance PDS activation by iron-based materials. In this study, a visible-light-responsive Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 heterojunction photocatalyst was synthesized to improve the ability of Fe2O3 for PDS activation through an effective Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle. Rapid tetracycline (TC, a common antibiotic) removal (91.7% within 60 min at a concentration of 20 mg·L-1) was achieved in the constructed Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system under conditions of 0.3 g·L-1 PDS and 0.3 g·L-1 catalyst. The corresponding pseudo-first-order rate constant reached 4.16 × 10-2 min- 1, which was 8.42 and 16.38 times higher than those of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/vis and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS systems, respectively. Moreover, the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 exhibited favorable stability and reusability even after five cycles. Lastly, a catalytic mechanism for TC removal and PDS activation was proposed based on quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests. The SO4 •
- and •OH played a major role in TC removal, while •O2
– and 1O2 contributed as auxiliary factors. This study presents a promising strategy for activating PDS using cost-effective and stable Fe(III)-based heterojunctions, and such an idea can be used to design catalysts for other high-value reactions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfate radical (SO4 •-)-based advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are promising technologies for wastewater purification due to their strong oxidizing ability (E (SO4 •-/SO4 2-) = 2.6–3.1 V) and extended lifespan (30–40 μs) [1,2]. Peroxydisulfate (PDS) can be activated to produce SO4 •- through various methods, e.g., heat, transition metals, ultraviolet light, ultrasound, electrolysis, and carbon materials [3–7]. Among these, Fe(II)-based materials are the most common due to their high efficiency and economic benefits [8,9]. However, a primary drawback of these materials is the easy oxidation of Fe(II) to Fe(III), which has a relatively poor ability to activate PDS (k = 6.6 × 10-2 M- 1·s- 1) [9]. Liu et al. reported that only 15% of PDS was decomposed over 30 days in the presence of 50 g/L ferrihydrite (Fe(OH)3(s)) [10]. Nevertheless, Fe(III)-based materials, such as Fe2O3, have advantages over Fe(II)-based materials, including greater stability, environmental availability, and lower cost. Thus, if an effective Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle can be established, Fe(III)-based materials could be superior catalysts for PDS activation.

To promote the catalytic efficiency of Fe(III)-based materials for PDS activation, hydroxylamine (HA) and ascorbic acid were used as the reductant to convert Fe(III) into Fe(II). For example, Tan et al. found that when HA was added to the Fe(II)/PDS system, the degradation of diuron significantly increased from 66% to 90% [11]. They confirmed that HA achieved an effective Fe(III) and Fe(II) cycle, which improved the PDS activation to produce more SO4 •-. Lei et al. used ascorbic acid as the reducing agent in Fe(III)/PDS system [12]. The modified system exhibited a wide working initial pH (pH0 = 2.0–6.2) and rapid and continuous oxidation of Acid Orange 7. However, these methods need to constantly replenish the reducing agents to maintain the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle, which inevitably increases the operating costs. Meanwhile, the extra organics will compete with reactive oxidative species (ROS) with target pollutants, possibly causing secondary pollution. Compared with ferrous salts, ferric salt is more stable and cheaper. Therefore, developing an economical, effective, and environmental-friendly method without an additional reducing agent to promote the PDS activation by Fe(III)-based materials is meaningful.

Previous studies have shown that the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle could realize through photo-assistant without external chemical consumption [13,14]. Compared with chemical-reducing agents, photo-assistant is more sustainable. Many photocatalysts have been used to form heterojunctions with Fe2O3 to improve their photo-Fenton and photo-Fenton-like performance. For example, Lee et al. found that hematite (α-Fe2O3)/graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanofilm composites could activate H2O2 under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation and achieved a 100% removal of methylene blue [15]. Demonstrated that the UV/PDS/S-α-Fe2O3 system had an excellent photocatalytic performance for carbamazepine (CBZ) removal due to the improved Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle by efficient electron-hole separation [16]. However, these materials only function under UV, producing relatively high energy consumption [17]. Thus, a photocatalyst with a broader response range, particularly one responsive to visible light, is highly desirable. Notably, Bi2WO6 is sensitive to both UV and visible light and exhibits satisfactory stability in practical applications [18,19]. However, no comparative research has been conducted on using Bi2WO6 to effectively activate PDS by Fe2O3 with photo assistance under visible light.

In this work, different Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composites were initially prepared using a hydrothermal method and subsequently used to activate PDS with visible light. Given the widespread use of tetracycline (TC) as a global antibiotic, its presence has been detected in groundwater and surface water at trace concentrations ranging from 11.16 ng·L-1 to 0.4 µg·L-1. Due to its persistence and bioaccumulation, TC poses a significant threat to human health and aquatic ecosystems [20]. Consequently, TC was chosen as a representative pollutant to assess the catalytic efficiency of the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system. Key factors, such as initial pH, co-existing anions, catalyst dose, PDS dose, and TC concentration, were also investigated. Additionally, the stability of this light-assisted PDS activation system was evaluated. The potential catalytic mechanism was proposed based on quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests. This research aims to provide new insights for PDS activation using low-cost and stable Fe(III)-based materials with photo-assistance.

Bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), ferric nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), sodium tungstate dehydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), sodium persulfate (Na2S2O8), tert-butanol (TBA), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), ethanol and methanol (MeOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline (TC) was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise specified, all chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and did not undergo further purification. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (>18.25 MΩ) from a Millipore Milli-Q water system.

As for single Bi2WO6, it was initially prepared using a hydrothermal synthesis method. First, 0.3299 g of Na2WO4·2H2O and 0.05 g of CTAB were dispersed in 60 mL of ultrapure water under sonication. Next, 0.9702 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added to the solution while stirring magnetically. The mixture was then placed in a 100 mL stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon lining and heated at 160 °C for 24 h. Finally, the white solid products were collected, washed three times with ethanol and deionized water, and dried overnight at 60 °C.

Synthesis of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 followed a similar procedure as above. First, 0.01 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 0.007 g of CH3COONa were dissolved in 60 mL of ethanol. Then, 0.17 g of Bi2WO6 was added to the mixed solution and stirred continuously using a magnetic stirrer for 8 h. The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 24 h. The resulting solid products were washed with ethanol and deionized water, and the samples, after suction filtration, were dried overnight at 60 °C. Bare Fe2O3 was prepared in the same way but without the addition of Bi2WO6.

The batch experiment was carried out under the irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp (PlS-FX300HU, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.) with a 420 nm cut filter (155 mW·cm- 2), and the removal of TC was used to evaluate the catalytic performance of the catalyst. A total of 0.06 g of the catalyst was added to a 200 mL TC solution (20 mg·L-1) and then stirred by a magnetic stirrer in the dark for 1 h to achieve the adsorption saturation. Afterward, the mixture was exposed to visible light, and 1 mL of PDS solution (60 g·L-1) was immediately added to the catalytic system. The temperature of the entire experiment was maintained at ∼ 25 °C using a circulating water system. Periodically, 2 mL of suspensions were sampled, filtered through a 0.22-μm micropore membrane filter, and immediately quenched by 100 μL of pure methanol. The reusability of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 under optimal reaction conditions was assessed through cycling experiments. After each reaction, the catalyst was collected by filtration, washed with ethanol and deionized water several times, and dried at 60 °C for subsequent experiments.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Ultima IV, Cu Kα radiation) analyses were performed with a scanning range from 10° to 90° to identify the components of the materials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer) was used to determine the surface elemental compositions with an Al Kα source. The morphology of the prepared catalysts was characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM). A transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were employed to examine the crystal lattice parameters. The materials' pore volume and specific surface area were determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analyzer, with a degassing temperature of 110 °C and a degassing time of 4 h. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) was utilized to analyze the material's infrared spectrum to determine the functional groups of composites. The material's ultraviolet, visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded using a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the 200–800 nm range. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signal spin-trapped by the reagent 5, 5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) and 2, 2, 6, 6-Tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TEMP) with an ESR spectrometer (ER200-SRC, Bruker Co., USA; Microwave frequency, 9.85 GHz; Center field, 3510 G; Sweep width, 100 G; Modulation frequency, 100 kHz; and Modulation amplitude, 1.00 G) was used to reconfirm the generated reactive species during the catalytic reaction. The photoelectrochemical performance of the as-prepared catalyst was analyzed using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation with a standard three-electrode system. The reference electrode was a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode, the counter electrode was a platinum wire, and the working electrode was an FTO glass coated with the catalyst. The measurements were conducted under open-circuit potential using 0.1 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte. A zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90) was used to determine the surface charge sample at different pH conditions.

TC concentration in the solution was measured using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile (C2H3N) and 0.1%(v/v) formic acid (HCOOH), and the volume ratio was 30/70. The injection time was 10 min, and the flow rate was 0.8 mL·min- 1. Triplicate experiments were performed, and average data were reported. The mineralization of TC was assessed by total organic carbon (TOC) using a TOC-5050A analyzer (Shimadzu VCHS/CSN, Japan). The degradation products were identified by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Agilent 1290 series). Mobile phase A was formic acid water with a volume fraction of 0.1%, and acetonitrile was mobile phase B. Elution was performed at a flow rate of 0.2 mL·min- 1. The gradient elution condition was 80 % A + 20 % B. The sample size was 5 μL, and the column temperature was 30 °C. The solvent removal gas was nitrogen, the flow rate was 10 mL·min- 1, and the gas temperature was 300 °C.

The pseudo-first-order kinetics (Eq. (1)) were utilized to calculate the rate constant.

where C t is the residual TC concentration at the reaction time of t min (mg·L-1), C 1 is the TC concentration (mg·L-1) after the dark reaction, and k obs are the pseudo-first-order rate constant (min- 1).

Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of Bi2WO6, Fe2O3, and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composite. Characteristic peaks located at 28.3°, 32.7°, 47.1°, 55.8°, 58.5°, 68.8°, 76.0°, and 78.3° could be found, corresponding to the (131), (200), (202), (133), (226), (040), (212), and (402) crystal planes of the orthorhombic Bi2WO6, respectively [21]. The diffraction peaks at 24.1°, 33.2°, 35.6°, 40.8°, 49.5°, 54.0°, 57.5°, 62.4°, and 64.0° corresponded to the (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (018), (214), and (300) planes of Fe2O3, respectively [22]. No characteristic peak of Fe2O3 was observed in the XRD spectra of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3, which could be due to the small proportion of Fe2O3 in the composite.

The surface chemical compositions and chemical states of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were identified by XPS characterization (Fig. 1b-f). As depicted in Fig. 1b, C, Bi, W, O, and Fe elements could be found in the survey XPS spectrum [23]. The two characteristic peaks of Bi 4f at 159.2 and 164.5 eV belonged to Bi 4f 7/2 and Bi 4f 5/2, respectively (Fig. 1c) [24]. Fig. 1d shows two characteristic peaks around 35.5 and 37.5 eV in the W 4f, corresponding to W 4f 7/2 and W 4f 5/2 of W6+, respectively [25]. Two distinct peaks of O 1s with the binding energies of 530.1 and 531.5 eV (Fig. 1e) corresponded to the surface lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen in the composite, respectively [24,26]. Moreover, the two peaks of Fe 2p appearing at 723.9 and 711.0 eV (Fig. 1f) confirmed the existence of Fe(III) [27,28]. These results indicate that Fe2O3 was successfully incorporated on the surface of Bi2WO6.

The morphology and microstructure of the prepared catalysts were analyzed by TEM, SEM, and HRTEM measurements. Fig. 2 a-c reveals that Bi2WO6, Fe2O3, and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 displayed nanoplate structures, while Fe2O3 showed obvious regular hexagonal morphologies. Based on the scale bar, their sizes were estimated to be approximately 40 nm, 50 nm, and 20 nm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2d-e, Fe2O3 covered the surface of Bi2WO6, indicating successful preparation of the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composite. The lattice fringes of the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composite could be clearly observed from the HRTEM image (Fig. 2f). The d-spacing values of the lattice fringes were 0.31 and 0.19 nm, which matched the (131) plane of Bi2WO6 and the (113) plane of Fe2O3 [24].

The specific surface areas of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were measured by BET analysis, and their N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 2g. The adsorption isotherms exhibited characteristics of type II and IV, indicating a mesoporous material. This observation was further supported by pore size analysis. Compared to Fe2O3 (8.20 m2·g- 1, 14.21 nm), Bi2WO6 (40.31 m2·g- 1, 16.45 nm), and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 (31.55 m2·g- 1, 15.01 nm) exhibited higher specific surface areas and larger pore sizes. This suggests that the composite materials offered more active sites for reactions, thereby enhancing the material's overall reactivity. The slight reduction in specific surface area and pore size observed after Fe2O3 loading might be attributed to partial blockage of the Bi2WO6 surface caused by the loaded Fe2O3 (Table S1, Fig S1).

The molecular structures of Bi2WO6, Fe2O3, and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 2h). A series of characteristic absorption peaks ranging from 500 to 2000 cm- 1 could be observed in the FT-IR spectra of Bi2WO6. The distinct absorption peaks at 579, 729, and 821 cm- 1 were attributed to the bridging stretching of Bi-O, W-O, and W-O-W in Bi2WO6, respectively [29,30]. The characteristic peaks below 500 cm- 1 were associated with the stretching and bending of Fe-O in Fe2O3 [31]. Notably, the prominent peaks of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were very similar to those of Bi2WO6, which could be assigned to the predominance of Bi2WO6 as a component in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composite.

The UV–vis DRS was used to determine the optical absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 2i, the photo-absorption wavelengths of Bi2WO6 and Fe2O3 were 420 nm and 645 nm, respectively. Due to the presence of Fe2O3, the absorption wavelength of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 (500 nm) showed a specific red shift compared with Bi2WO6, indicating that the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composite was more effective under visible light [22]. The band gaps (3.38, 3.20, and 2.36 eV, respectively) and the conduction band (CB) positions (-0.47, -0.57, and -0.50 eV, respectively) of Bi2WO6, Fe2O3, and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were determined using the Tauc plot and Mott-Schottky plot, respectively. Therefore, their valence band (VB) positions were estimated to be 2.91 eV, 1.79 eV, and 2.70 eV, respectively (Fig S2). The higher CB potential and lower VB potential of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 compared to Bi2WO6 suggest the possible formation of a favorable heterojunction between Fe2O3 and Bi2WO6. Such a formed heterojunction facilitated the generation and transfer of photo-generated carriers, enhancing the Fe(II)/Fe(III) cycle [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible-light-assisted activation PDS using Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of TC evaluated the catalytic performance of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 for PDS activation under visible light. Adsorption results showed that an adsorption–desorption equilibrium could be achieved within 60 min under dark reaction conditions. Thus, subsequent experiments were stirred in the dark for 60 min to exclude the absorption. Meanwhile, the TC removal by PDS alone was negligible under visible light (4.5%). Fig. 3 a, c and e show that the TC adsorption removals by Bi2WO6, Fe2O3, and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were 14.6%, 6.9%, and 19.9%, respectively. This suggests that an enhanced absorption might favor the subsequent catalytic degradation. With the introduction of PDS or visible light, TC removal performance varied significantly. In the absence of PDS as an electron acceptor, the removal of TC by Fe2O3 under visible light was only 9.1% within 1 h, suggesting that Fe2O3 could hardly respond to visible light. The efficiency of Bi2WO6 for TC removal under visible light increased to 32.4%, which was lower than that of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 (42.8%). This could be attributed to the heterojunction structure of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3, which improved its utilization efficiency of visible light. Although bare Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 exhibited a certain photocatalytic capability, its performance was unsatisfactory. Meanwhile, the catalytic activities of Bi2WO6, Fe2O3, and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were poor, with corresponding TC removal rates of 18.1%, 10.7%, and 31.6% in the Bi2WO6/PDS, Fe2O3/PDS, and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS systems, respectively. These results suggest a combined effect could work between Bi2WO6/Fe2O3, PDS, and visible light.

As shown in Fig. S3 and Table S2, TC removal in Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system could reach 91.7% within 60 min, higher than Fe2O3/PDS/vis (39.7%) and Bi2WO6/PDS/vis (56.3%). These results indicated that although Bi2WO6 had a specific ability for direct TC removal or photocatalytic activation of PDS for TC removal, Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 exhibited better performance for PDS activation in the presence of visible light. The rate constant of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis was 4.16 × 10-2 min- 1, which was much higher than other systems (Table S2). Furthermore, compared to the Bi2WO6/PDS/vis (8.7%) and Fe2O3/PDS/vis (6.5%) system, the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system (12.3%) exhibited the highest PDS utilization rate (Fig S4). Thus, Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 was a feasible and highly-effective PDS activator under visible light irradiation and exhibited better application potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameter impact survey of the constructed light-assisted PDS system
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of influencing factors, including Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 dosage, PDS dosage, and initial pollutant concentration, on TC removal by Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system, was investigated (Fig. 4 , Table S3). As shown in Fig. 4a, increasing the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 dosage (0.1-0.3 g·L-1) initially had a noticeable strengthening effect on TC degradation. The additional catalyst provided more active sites for PDS activation, generating more reactive oxygen species (ROS) and favoring pollutant removal. However, when the dose exceeded 0.3 g·L-1, the increase in TC removal after 60 min was insignificant. This could be attributed to the excess solid catalyst (Bi2WO6/Fe2O3) in the solution, causing cloudiness, reducing visible light intensity reaching the bottom solution, and suppressing the photo-assistant action [34]. The corresponding rate constant in Table S3 also exhibited a similar trend. Fig. 4b demonstrated that lower initial TC concentrations rapidly achieved high TC removal in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system. This could be attributed to the decrease in the production of intermediate products when the initial concentration of pollutants was reduced. Consequently, the competitive effect between TC molecules and deuterogenic by-products for limited ROS was weakened [35]. The impact of PDS dosage on TC removal is presented in Fig. 4c. The influence of PDS dosage was similar to the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 dosage. TC removal was significantly promoted by increasing the PDS dose within the 0.1-0.4 g·L-1. However, the TC removal efficiency decreased when the PDS dosage exceeded 0.6 g·L-1.

Three reasons could explain these phenomena. Firstly, the excess PDS could scavenge the generated SO4 •
- (Eq. (2)), thereby reducing the amount of ROS and suppressing the TC removal [36]. Secondly, excessive SO4 •
- could undergo a self-quenching reaction in the solution (Eq. (3)) [36]. Thirdly, excessive PDS could compete with TC for the active sites on the surface of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3, resulting in a decrease in the removal efficiency of TC.

In practical applications, the pH level, co-existing anions, and the presence of natural organic matter can significantly influence the performance of AOPs. Thus, their possible influences were also explored in this study. The effect of different pH values on TC removal was examined by varying the initial pH levels at 3.0, 4.5 (natural pH), 5.0, 7.0, and 9.0 (Fig. 5 a, c, and Table S4). Under weak acidic conditions, the pH of the solution remained relatively stable throughout the reaction, and the TC removal rate was the highest, approximately 0.042 min- 1. In contrast, under alkaline conditions, the pH of the solution decreased significantly during the reaction, resulting in the inhibition of TC removal (Fig S5).

Based on the zeta potentials (Fig. S6), the isoelectric point of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 was around pH = 7.0. Zeta potential results also indicated that the surface of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 was positively charged in an acidic environment. Therefore, this facilitated the adsorption of S2O8 2-, thereby generating more SO4 •
- . Additionally, previous studies have suggested several mechanisms to explain the effect of initial pH on PDS activation, which applied to this study: (i) Acidic conditions promote the production of more soluble Fe(II) [37], accelerating PDS activation and generating a large amount of SO4 •
-
[38]. (ii) Alkaline conditions lead to the conversion of a small portion of Fe(III) into Fe(OH)3 [39], rendering subsequent reactions impossible and reducing the activation of PDS and the degradation rate of TC. (iii) Under strongly alkaline conditions, SO4 •
- was more likely to undergo a radical transformation to form •OH [40].

In Fig. 5b, d and Table S5, the presence of co-existing anions at 10 mM, such as Cl - , SO4 2
- , and NO3
– , had a certain inhibitory effect on the degradation of TC in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system. After 60 min, the TC removal decreased by 21.7%, 21.9%, and 11.7% for Cl - , SO4 2
- , and NO3
– , respectively. The negative influence of these co-existing anions could be attributed to the positive surface charge of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3. Adding anions may occupy the active catalytic sites on the surface of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 through electrostatic adsorption, thereby inhibiting PDS activation and subsequently affecting TC degradation. Besides, Cl- might generate weak oxidative chlorine radicals such as Cl2 •- (E 0 = 2.0 V) and Cl• (E 0 = 2.4 V) through trapping sulfate and hydroxyl radicals according to Eqs. (4)–(5) [41,42].

Moreover, the influence of humic acid (HA), a representative natural organic matter, on the removal of TC in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system was investigated (Fig S7). Within a low concentration range of HA (5–20 mg·L-1), TC exhibited a relatively high removal efficiency. Even at high HA concentrations (40 mg·L-1), the inhibition of TC removal was only approximately 19%. These findings highlight the robust environmental adaptability and interference resistance of the constructed Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system.

To evaluate its practical application, a comprehensive techno-economic analysis was conducted on the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system. Economic calculations based on raw materials and treatment capacity revealed that this study's developed water treatment technology exhibited a remarkably low cost of only 0.022 USD/m3S, substantially lower than other techniques for organic pollutant removal from water (Table S6) [43–47]. Moreover, this study demonstrated higher removal efficiency than similar water treatment technologies (Table S7) [48–55]. The combination of lower cost and faster reaction rate highlights the significant economic benefits and practical application potential of the technology developed in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the heterojunction-activated PDS system under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The stability and reusability of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 for PDS activation under visible light irradiation were evaluated through cycling experiments. Fig. 6 a shows that the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 maintained high reactivity in activating PDS after 5 cycles. Specifically, no visible reduction was observed. XRD, XPS, and FT-IR analyses indicated that the chemical composition, molecular structure, and corresponding atomic ratios of the fresh and used Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 remained essentially unchanged (Fig. 6b-d). However, a slight decrease in the catalyst's adsorption performance was observed after the first cycle experiment, likely due to the blockage of tiny pores in the composite material. This could explain the slight decrease in TC removal. Overall, even after multiple cycles, Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 showed excellent stability and reusability for PDS activation under visible light. After five cycles, the total iron leaching of the original catalyst was only 0.9% (0.2442 mg·L-1), indicating sustained activity and negligible loss of the catalytic material (Table S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mineralization ability and TC removal pathways
ce:section.ce:para: The mineralization rate of TC (63.5%) was found to be lower than the total TC removal (91.7%) in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system (Fig. 4d), indicating the formation of by-products during the degradation process. Table S9 shows the LC-MS results, identifying twelve intermediates and three final products. The peak corresponding to TC (m/z = 445) disappeared after the reaction, indicating its degradation. Based on previous reports, the removal of TC involved three potential pathways (Fig. 7 a) [8,56,57]. Initially, the action of free radicals and holes led to the loss of dimethyl amino groups, resulting in the formation of products P1 (m/z = 433), P6 (m/z = 417), and P9 (m/z = 432) [58]. The dehydroxylation and deamination of P1 contributed to the formation of products P2 (m/z = 416) and P3 (m/z = 403) [56]. The dehydration of P6 resulted in product P7 (m/z = 401). Moreover, the product P10 (m/z = 396) was formed through the dealkylation and dehydration reaction of P9 with h+ [59,60]. Free radical attacks further formed product P4 (m/z = 345) [56]. Ring-opening reactions gave rise to the production of products P5 (m/z = 319), P8 (m/z = 332), P11 (m/z = 364), and P12 (m/z = 279) [57]. Finally, all the intermediates were decomposed into smaller substances, such as F1 (m/z = 274), F2 (m/z = 230), F3 (m/z = 120), F4 (m/z = 118), ultimately resulting in complete mineralization into H2O and CO2 [8].

To better understand the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PS/vis system's potential, we further analyzed the by-products of TC removal using the Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (Fig. 7b, c and Table S10). This model predicted the toxicity of the intermediates. The results indicated that tetracycline was progressively transformed from a highly toxic state to a less toxic one during the reaction. These findings confirm that the photo-assisted PDS activation facilitated simultaneous degradation, mineralization, and detoxification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The underlying catalytic mechanisms in Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system
ce:section.ce:para:
To identify the major ROS involved in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system, quenching experiments were conducted using different scavengers, namely methanol (MeOH), tert-butanol (TBA), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), p-benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7). The results of these experiments are summarized as follows: MeOH was found to effectively quench both SO4 •
- and •OH, with reaction rate constants of k SO4•
-
/MeOH = 2.5 × 107 M- 1·s- 1, k
·
OH/MeOH = 9.7 × 108 M- 1·s- 1, respectively [35,61]. TBA demonstrated a significantly higher reaction rate with •OH compared to SO4 •
- , with rate constants of k SO4•
-
/TBA = 4.0 × 105-9.1 × 105 M- 1·s- 1 and k
·
OH/TBA = 3.8 × 108-7.6 × 108 M- 1·s- 1) [35,62]. Therefore, TBA effectively scavenged •OH but not SO4 •
- . BQ was found to be a quencher for •O2
– , with a reaction rate constant of k O2•
-
/BQ = 1.0 × 109 M- 1·s- 1 [8,63,64]. FFA was observed to quench 1O2, with a reaction rate constant of k 1O2 /FFA = 1.2 × 108 M- 1·s- 1 [65].

These quenching experiments provided valuable insights into the role of different ROS, e.g., SO4 •
- , •OH, •O2
–, and 1O2, in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system and helped to elucidate the predominant ROS responsible for the degradation process.

Fig. 8 a, b demonstrate the inhibitory effects of different quenchers on TC removal in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system. When MeOH and TBA were added at varying concentrations (10–1000 mM), they both exhibited inhibitory effects on the reaction (Fig. 8b). These findings indicate the involvement of •OH and SO4 •
- in the degradation of TC in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system. The inhibitory effects became more pronounced with increasing concentrations of MeOH and TBA. The addition of 1000 mM MeOH and 1000 mM TBA resulted in a decrease in the removal efficiency of TC from 91.7% to 48.4% and 67.7%, respectively. This observation emphasizes the dominant role of SO4 •
- radicals and •OH radicals in degrading organic pollutants. Furthermore, adding 2 mM BQ led to a 16.4% reduction (from 91.7% to 75.3%) in TC removal within 60 min, indicating the involvement of •O2
– . Surprisingly, adding 2 mM FFA decreased the TC removal efficiency to 71.5%, indicating the participation 1O2 in TC removal in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system. Furthermore, EDTA and K2Cr2O7 were used as holes (h+) and electrons (e-) scavengers, respectively, to study the photocatalytic activity of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 [35,66]. Fig. 8a shows that EDTA and K2Cr2O7 significantly quenched TC removal. In the presence of 2 mM EDTA and K2Cr2O7, the TC removal decreased substantially from 91.7% to 60.4% and 57.3%, respectively. These results indicated that the h+ and e- generated by photocatalysis in Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were quenched, and the electron-hole pairs generated by the catalyst also contributed to pollutant removal. The effect of K2Cr2O7 was slightly more pronounced, possibly due to the quenching of electrons, which inhibited the production of Fe(II) and resulted in a decrease in SO4 •
- . In addition, various electrochemical techniques, including transient photocurrent, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and linear scan voltammetry were employed to analyze different samples (Fig S8-S10). The results revealed that Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 exhibited a stronger photocurrent response, lower charge transfer resistance, and higher electron accumulation than Bi2WO6 and Fe2O3. These findings indicate the superior photo-response ability and efficient electron transfer characteristics of the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3. Furthermore, the i-t curves exhibited pronounced changes in current for Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 when PDS and TC were added, suggesting that the heterojunction structure significantly enhanced PDS activation and facilitated TC removal (Fig S11). Based on the VB value, the generation and transfer mechanism of electron-hole pairs in Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 in response to visible light can be summarized as follows:

Under visible light irradiation, e- in the oxidation catalyst Bi2WO6, located at a lower conduction band (CB), were excited. These electrons were then transferred to the higher CB of the Fe2O3 through the heterojunction formed between Bi2WO6 and Fe2O3. Simultaneously, the h+ in the VB of Bi2WO6 and e- in the CB of Fe2O3 could initiate catalytic oxidation and reduction reactions [67,68]. This synergistic electron transfer and charge separation process at the heterojunction interface played a crucial role in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composite, further favoring PDS activation.

ESR tests were conducted to investigate the role of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in TC degradation to detect active species. In the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system, characteristic signals of the DMPO-SO4 •
- adducts (1:1:1:1:1:1) and the DMPO-•OH adducts (1:2:2:1) were observed (Fig. 8c). The signal intensities at 5 min were more pronounced than at 1 min, indicating the generation of •OH and SO4 •
- through different pathways. These findings confirm the participation of both •OH and SO4 •
- in TC removal. Fig. 8d shows the appearance of DMPO-•O2
– signal after the light was turned on, indicating the presence of •O2
– . Fig. 8e further revealed the presence of 1O2 during the degradation. Thus, SO4 •
- , •OH, •O2
– , and 1O2 contributed to TC removal, consistent with previous quenching experiments' results.

Based on the above analyses, a proposed catalytic mechanism for PDS activation by Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 under visible light is depicted in Fig. 8f. Upon visible light irradiation, the photo-generated electrons in Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 were excited, and transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), resulting in the generation of h+ in the VB and e- in the CB (Eq. (6)). Due to the built-in electric field, recombination between the e- and h+ was inhibited, leading to the accumulation of e- in the CB and holes in the VB, thus enhancing the separation efficiency of e--h+ pairs and improving the catalytic performance of Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 [22]. The accumulated e- could activate O2 to form •O2
– , while h+ in the VB might react with OH – to form •OH (Eqs. (7)–(8) [69–71]. The experimental results also indicate that visible light has a weak activation effect on PDS (Eq. (9)). Excited e- in the photocatalyst could also activate a small amount of PDS to produce SO4 •
- (Eq. (10)). Simultaneously, the consumption of e- by PDS also promoted the separation efficiency of e--h+ pairs.

XPS characterization reveals the appearance of a new Fe peak at 709.5 eV in the used Bi2WO6/Fe2O3, suggesting the formation of Fe(II) during the reaction, possibly resulting from the conversion of Fe(III) (Fig. S12). Previous studies have shown that Fe(III) can act as a trap for photo-generated electrons, suppressing the recombination of h+ and e- [72]. The Fe(III) could be reduced to Fe(II) by e- in the CB, and Fe(II) could react with h+ to regenerate Fe(III) (Eqs. (11)–(12)). This cyclic transition of the valence state of Fe under visible light irradiation activates PDS and enhances the material's durability, thereby improving the removal efficiency of organic pollutants. Compared to the Bi2WO6/vis system (4.44 × 10-3 min- 1), the TC removal rate was increased by approximately 2.39 times in the Bi2WO6/PDS/vis system (1.06 × 10-2 min- 1). Similarly, compared to the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/vis system (4.94 × 10-3 min- 1), the TC removal rate was increased by ∼ 8.42 times in the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system (4.16 × 10-2 min- 1). The stronger enhancement effect provided further evidence that adding Fe promoted PDS activation and improved organic pollutants' removal efficiency.

Based on the quenching experiments and ESR analysis, it could be proposed that the TC removal induced by PDS activation involved two mechanisms: radical and non-radical pathways. The radical pathways primarily involved Fenton-like reactions Eq. (13), where PDS was activated by Fe(II) to produce SO4 •
- . In addition, the decomposition of PDS in water can generate a small amount of SO4 •
- and •O2
– through radical reactions Eq. (14) [73]. The non-radical pathway followed Eq. (15), where •O2
– radicals in the solution were an intermediate to generate 1O2, contributing to TC removal [74–76].

This study synthesized a highly efficient and environmentally friendly composite material, Bi2WO6/Fe2O3, and demonstrated its excellent performance in PDS activation under visible light. Compared to single photocatalysis or other AOPs, the constructed Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system exhibited remarkable efficiency in degrading TC. The reaction rate increase of ∼ 8.42 and 16.38 times higher than Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/vis and Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS systems was thus achieved. The incorporation of PDS effectively facilitated the separation of photo-generated e--h+ pairs in Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 under visible light irradiation. Meanwhile, the Bi2WO6/Fe2O3 composite demonstrated a significant capability in accelerating the conversion between Fe(III) and Fe(II), enhancing PDS activation and ultimately promoting TC removal.

The developed Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system demonstrated excellent performance across a broad pH range, showing its applicability in various environmental conditions. Furthermore, the system resisted interference from co-existing cations and organic matter, ensuring its effectiveness in complex water matrices. Additionally, the economic analysis revealed that the system has extremely low operating costs, making it a cost-effective solution for water treatment. Besides, cyclic experiments confirmed Bi2WO6/Fe2O3/PDS/vis system stability. Quenching experiments and ESR analysis provided evidence for the involvement of SO4 •
- , •OH, •O2
–, and 1O2 in the degradation of TC. These findings highlight the potential of Fe(III)-based materials in PDS-based advanced oxidation technologies. Such a designed system provides valuable insights into applying Fe(III)-based materials in efficient and sustainable wastewater treatment processes, specifically for addressing antibiotic-based pollution. This work highlights the synergistic effect of heterojunctions and presents new ideas for enhancing the activity of monomeric catalysts. The findings contribute to the development of innovative approaches for improving water treatment and pollution remediation.

Qi Yang and Fei Chen conceived and planned the experiments, and Yu-Jie Liu and Chang-Wei Bai performed the related experiments. Yu-Jie Liu and Chang-Wei Bai conducted, and Yi-Jiao Sun, Kun-Jie Hou, Qi Yang, and Fei Chen assisted in collecting data and analyzing various characterizations. Qi Yang, Yu-Lie Liu, Chang-Wei Bai, and Fei Chen wrote and revised the manuscript.
